intermediate_messages.expected_action: Create a concise execution plan and deliverable structure.
intermediate_messages.content:
1. System Overview
- Purpose: A backend service that accepts PDF, TXT, and Markdown files, validates and persists originals and metadata, extracts text, segments content into searchable units, builds and maintains a search index, supports document search, and reports processing status. It supports asynchronous processing, retryable steps, idempotent duplicate handling, and easy extension to new formats.
- Core goals: reliable ingestion, scalable text extraction, robust indexing, responsive search API, clear status reporting, and maintainable codebase for a small team.

2. Main Components and Responsibilities
- Ingestion API Gateway
  - Input: file upload request (multipart or base64), metadata, user id.
  - Output: acceptance acknowledgement and processing job id.
- Validation Service
  - Validate file type, size, and metadata constraints.
  - Reject unsupported formats early; normalize metadata.
- Storage Layer
  - Persist original file and metadata (immutable once stored).
  - Store per-document and per-section indexing data.
- Document Processor (Asynchronous Worker)
  - Extract text via format-specific parsers.
  - Segment text into searchable units (sections, paragraphs, tokens as needed).
  - Persist extracted text and per-section metadata.
- Indexing Service
  - Build and maintain a searchable index (e.g., inverted index, metadata fields).
  - Support incremental re-indexing on updates.
- Search Service
  - Receive query, return ranked document/section results with snippets.
- Status & Telemetry Service
  - Track job states (queued, processing, failed, completed).
  - Retry logic, failure reasons, retry backoff, and alert hooks.
- Deduplication & Idempotency Layer
  - Detect repeated uploads of the same content (by content hash) and avoid duplicate document records.
- Format Adapters
  - Plug-in architecture to add new document formats with a common interface:
    - validate(file)
    - extractText(file)
    - segmentText(text) -> list of segments
- API & Auth Layer
  - REST endpoints, authentication/authorization hooks, rate limiting.
- Admin/Monitoring UI (optional)
  - Health, job status, and basic search demos for developers.

3. End-to-End Processing Workflow
- Client uploads file with metadata.
- Ingestion API validates request, computes content hash, and checks for duplicates.
  - If duplicate exists and still valid, return existing document id and status.
  - If not duplicate, create a document record in a “queued” state and enqueue a processing job.
- Validation Service ensures file size, mime type, and metadata constraints.
- Storage Layer saves original file and metadata atomically.
- Document Processor:
  - Selects appropriate Format Adapter based on file type.
  - Extracts text; on failure, emits a retriable error.
  - Segments text into searchable units with positions and metadata.
  - Stores extracted text and segment records.
- Indexing Service builds/updates index for the document and its segments.
- Status & Telemetry Service updates job state to completed or failed with retry policies.
- Search Service responds to search queries by querying the index and returning ranked results with snippets and document metadata.

4. REST API Design
- Endpoints (brief): 
- POST /documents
  - Purpose: Upload a new document.
  - Request Data: multipart/form-data or JSON with base64; fields: userId, title, tags, expiration, file (binary), fileName, mimeType, size.
  - Response Data: { documentId, status: "queued" | "processing" | "completed" | "failed", jobId, message }
- GET /documents/{documentId}
  - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and processing status.
  - Response Data: { documentId, title, size, mimeType, status, createdAt, updatedAt, tags, hash }
- GET /documents/{documentId}/contents
  - Purpose: Retrieve text segments or full extracted text (limited/paginated as needed).
  - Response Data: { segments: [ { id, range, text, metadata } ], totalSegments }
- GET /documents/{documentId}/search?query=...
  - Purpose: Search within a document or across index (global search if allowed).
  - Request Data: query string; optional parameters: page, size, sort
  - Response Data: { results: [ { documentId, segmentId, score, snippet, metadata } ], total }
- GET /jobs/{jobId}
  - Purpose: Check processing status and logs.
  - Response Data: { jobId, status, progress, lastError, retryCount, timestamps }
- Optional:
- DELETE /documents/{documentId}
  - Purpose: Soft-delete or archive document.

5. Data Model
- Document
  - documentId (UUID)
  - userId
  - title
  - hash (content hash for dedup)
  - mimeType
  - size
  - createdAt, updatedAt
  - status (queued, processing, completed, failed)
  - metadata (JSON blob)
- OriginalFile
  - documentId (FK)
  - storagePath (object store path or blob reference)
  - storageKey
- TextContent
  - documentId (FK)
  - contentId (UUID)
  - text
  - language (if detected)
- Segment
  - segmentId (UUID)
  - documentId (FK)
  - indexPosition
  - text
  - startOffset, endOffset (character positions)
  - metadata (e.g., section heading)
- IndexEntry (per term)
  - term
  - documentId
  - segmentId
  - frequency
  - positionList (optional)
- Job
  - jobId (UUID)
  - documentId (FK)
  - status
  - stage
  - createdAt, updatedAt
  - retryCount
  - lastError

6. Suggested Project Structure
- src/
  - api/
    - routes for REST endpoints
    - request/response schemas
  - services/
    - ingestion.go, validation.go
    - storage.go (file/metadata persistence)
    - processors/
      - format_adapters/
        - pdf.go, txt.go, md.go (interfaces: validate, extractText, segmentText)
      - text_extractor.go (coordinator)
    - indexing/
      - indexer.go, searcher.go
    - jobs/
      - queue.go, worker.go, retry.go
    - status.go
  - models/
    - document.go, segment.go, index.go, job.go
  - config/
    - settings.go (env/config)
  - tests/
    - unit and integration tests stubs
  - utils/
    - hashing.go, storage_paths.go
- cmd/
  - api-server/
  - worker-service/
- docs/
  - design.md, API contract

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Idempotent Uploads:
  - Compute content hash on upload; if a document with same hash exists and is not deleted, return existing documentId with current status.
  - Use upsert semantics for document records to prevent duplicates.
- Retries:
  - Retries for transient failures (network, parsers) with exponential backoff (e.g., 1s, 2s, 4s, up to a max).
  - Distinguish retryable vs non-retryable errors; non-retryable (invalid file content) fail early with clear status.
- Failure Handling:
  - Persist lastError and retryCount in Job.
  - Dead-letter or alert if retryLimit reached.
- Validation Failures:
  - Immediate failure with actionable messages; do not enqueue.
- Idempotent Processing Steps:
  - Each stage writes idempotent records keyed by documentId/segmentId; reprocessing uses existing keys to avoid duplicates.

8. Testing Plan
- Unit Tests:
  - Format adapters: parse and extract for sample PDFs, TXT, MD.
  - Hashing and deduplication logic.
  - Segmenting rules and snippet generation.
- Integration Tests:
  - End-to-end upload for each supported format with a mocked storage/index layer.
  - Validate status transitions: queued -> processing -> completed.
  - Test retry paths by simulating transient failures.
- Property/Contract Tests:
  - API input validation, required fields, and error responses.
- Performance/Load Tests:
  - Basic load tests with concurrent uploads and search queries to ensure responsiveness.
- Acceptance Criteria:
  - Duplicate uploads are deduplicated.
  - All file types processed with correct text extraction and indexing.
  - Search returns relevant results with accurate snippets.

9. Design Tradeoff
- Tradeoff: Rich per-segment indexing versus simplicity.
  - Chosen: Index at a per-segment granularity to support precise search results and meaningful snippets, accepting higher indexing complexity and storage. Rationale: users expect document-level and section-level search capabilities; per-segment indexing enables more relevant ranking and snippet generation, at the cost of additional storage and compute during indexing. Alternative is document-level indexing only, which would simplify storage but reduce search granularity and result quality.

Deliverable Summary
- A modular backend architecture with asynchronous processing, robust deduplication, and extensible format adapters.
- Clear data model supporting documents, segments, and index entries.
- REST API contracts for upload, status, search, and retrieval.
- Planned project structure emphasizing maintainability for a small team.
- Defined error handling, retry strategy, and a concise testing plan.
- One design tradeoff highlighted to guide future evolution.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Synthesize upstream work into conclusions tied to the task requirements.
intermediate_messages.content:
Below is a concise, decision-grounded synthesis of the proposed design, tied directly to the task requirements. It emphasizes constraints, explicit calculations, and actionable rationale for maintainability, extensibility, and reliability.

Grounded conclusions by requirement

1) Ingestion, validation, storage, and processing
- Conclusion: A core, asynchronous pipeline with idempotent deduplication best satisfies reliability and duplicate-prevention constraints. Computing a content hash on upload enables safe upsert behavior: if a duplicate exists (and is not deleted), the system can return the existing documentId and current status without reprocessing. This directly addresses the “prevent unnecessary duplicate records” constraint.
- Key implication: Treat upload as a two-phase decision (dedupe check, then enqueue if new). Ensure the hash is computed deterministically (e.g., SHA-256 of raw bytes) and stored alongside metadata for idempotent retry behavior.

2) Supported formats and extensibility
- Conclusion: A plug-in Format Adapters abstraction (validate, extractText, segmentText) provides clean separation of concerns and enables flat addition of new formats with minimal risk to existing code. The three current adapters (PDF, TXT, Markdown) satisfy the required inputs; adding new formats later becomes a straightforward extension.
- Design impact: Adapters should expose a stable interface; the orchestrator uses the adapter determined by mimeType or file extension. This reduces branching and keeps maintenance scope small for a small team.

3) Text extraction, segmentation, and indexing
- Conclusion: Per-segment indexing (not just document-level) improves search quality and snippet relevance, aligning with user expectations for precise results. Although it increases indexing work and storage, the benefits for search UX justify the tradeoff.
- Implication: Segment metadata (range, position, possibly section headings) should be lightweight yet sufficient for ranking and snippet generation. Store per-segment index entries to support fast neighborhood queries during search.

4) End-to-end processing workflow
- Conclusion: An explicit job-based workflow with a Status & Telemetry Service is required to provide clear progress indicators and robust retry semantics. Distinguishing transient vs non-transient errors allows safe backoffs and prevents unnecessary retries on invalid content.
- Operational note: A dead-letter path or alert should trigger when retry limits are exceeded to avoid silent failures.

5) REST API design
- Conclusion: The API surface covers ingestion, status, content access, and search with a reasonable separation of concerns:
  - POST /documents for ingestion
  - GET /documents/{documentId} for metadata/status
  - GET /documents/{documentId}/contents for extracted text/segments
  - GET /documents/{documentId}/search for in-document search
  - GET /jobs/{jobId} for processing telemetry
- Rationale: This design satisfies the requirement to report processing status and support document search while remaining approachable for a small team. Optional delete/archive endpoints can be included later if needed.

6) Data model
- Conclusion: Data models align with requirements and balance clarity with maintainability:
  - Document, OriginalFile, TextContent, Segment, IndexEntry, Job
- Important constraints validated:
  - Hash field enables deduplication
  - Status field drives UI and API responses
  - Segment supports per-section granularity
- Futureproofing: Metadata stored as JSON enables easy extension for new formats without schema churn.

7) Project structure and maintainability
- Conclusion: A modular repo layout with clear boundaries between api, services, processors, indexing, jobs, and models supports small-team maintainability. The “format_adapters” directory provides a clean extensibility path for new formats with minimal cross-module impact.
- Maintaining simplicity: Keep processor logic stateless between worker restarts where feasible; rely on idempotent writes for safe retries.

8) Error handling, retry, and duplicate prevention
- Conclusion: The following mechanisms are essential and sufficient to satisfy constraints:
  - Idempotent uploads via content hash and upsert semantics
  - Distinguish retryable vs non-retryable errors with exponential backoff
  - Persist lastError, retryCount, and provide a dead-letter path at max retries
  - Validation failures fail fast with actionable messages (no enqueue)
- Operational guardrails: Ensure retry loops are bounded and have visibility through /jobs/{jobId} endpoints for operators.

9) Testing plan
- Conclusion: A layered testing plan is essential to maintain quality with a small team:
  - Unit tests for adapters, hashing, and segmenting
  - Integration tests for end-to-end flow with mocked storage/index layers
  - Contract tests for REST API inputs/outputs
  - Load testing to validate concurrency behavior and search latency
- Rationale: Early, targeted tests reduce regression risk as the team scales.

10) Design tradeoff
- Conclusion: Per-segment indexing is chosen over document-level indexing to improve snippet quality and ranking precision. This tradeoff increases indexing complexity and storage and is justified by user-centered search quality requirements.
- Alternative acknowledged: Document-level indexing would simplify implementation but degrade user experience for granular searches.

Overall recommendation
- Proceed with the described architecture as a modular backend with asynchronous processing, robust deduplication, and extensible format adapters. Keep the initial scope tight (PDF/TXT/Markdown adapters, moderate segment sizing, and a straightforward index) to deliver a maintainable baseline for a small team, with a clear path to extend formats, adjust segment granularity, and enhance indexing strategies as needed.

  - Stores extracted text and segment records.
- Indexing Service builds/updates index for the document and its segments.
- Status & Telemetry Service updates job state to completed or failed with retry policies.
- Search Service responds to search queries by querying the index and returning ranked results with snippets and document metadata.

4. REST API Design
- Endpoints (brief): 
- POST /documents
  - Purpose: Upload a new document.
  - Request Data: multipart/form-data or JSON with base64; fields: userId, title, tags, expiration, file (binary), fileName, mimeType, size.
  - Response Data: { documentId, status: "queued" | "processing" | "completed" | "failed", jobId, message }
- GET /documents/{documentId}
  - Purpose: Retrieve document metadata and processing status.
  - Response Data: { documentId, title, size, mimeType, status, createdAt, updatedAt, tags, hash }
- GET /documents/{documentId}/contents
  - Purpose: Retrieve text segments or full extracted text (limited/paginated as needed).
  - Response Data: { segments: [ { id, range, text, metadata } ], totalSegments }
- GET /documents/{documentId}/search?query=...
  - Purpose: Search within a document or across index (global search if allowed).
  - Request Data: query string; optional parameters: page, size, sort
  - Response Data: { results: [ { documentId, segmentId, score, snippet, metadata } ], total }
- GET /jobs/{jobId}
  - Purpose: Check processing status and logs.
  - Response Data: { jobId, status, progress, lastError, retryCount, timestamps }
- Optional:
- DELETE /documents/{documentId}
  - Purpose: Soft-delete or archive document.

5. Data Model
- Document
  - documentId (UUID)
  - userId
  - title
  - hash (content hash for dedup)
  - mimeType
  - size
  - createdAt, updatedAt
  - status (queued, processing, completed, failed)
  - metadata (JSON blob)
- OriginalFile
  - documentId (FK)
  - storagePath (object store path or blob reference)
  - storageKey
- TextContent
  - documentId (FK)
  - contentId (UUID)
  - text
  - language (if detected)
- Segment
  - segmentId (UUID)
  - documentId (FK)
  - indexPosition
  - text
  - startOffset, endOffset (character positions)
  - metadata (e.g., section heading)
- IndexEntry (per term)
  - term
  - documentId
  - segmentId
  - frequency
  - positionList (optional)
- Job
  - jobId (UUID)
  - documentId (FK)
  - status
  - stage
  - createdAt, updatedAt
  - retryCount
  - lastError

6. Suggested Project Structure
- src/
  - api/
    - routes for REST endpoints
    - request/response schemas
  - services/
    - ingestion.go, validation.go
    - storage.go (file/metadata persistence)
    - processors/
      - format_adapters/
        - pdf.go, txt.go, md.go (interfaces: validate, extractText, segmentText)
      - text_extractor.go (coordinator)
    - indexing/
      - indexer.go, searcher.go
    - jobs/
      - queue.go, worker.go, retry.go
    - status.go
  - models/
    - document.go, segment.go, index.go, job.go
  - config/
    - settings.go (env/config)
  - tests/
    - unit and integration tests stubs
  - utils/
    - hashing.go, storage_paths.go
- cmd/
  - api-server/
  - worker-service/
- docs/
  - design.md, API contract

7. Error Handling, Retry, and Duplicate Prevention
- Idempotent Uploads:
  - Compute content hash on upload; if a document with same hash exists and is not deleted, return existing documentId with current status.
  - Use upsert semantics for document records to prevent duplicates.
- Retries:
  - Retries for transient failures (network, parsers) with exponential backoff (e.g., 1s, 2s, 4s, up to a max).
  - Distinguish retryable vs non-retryable errors; non-retryable (invalid file content) fail early with clear status.
- Failure Handling:
  - Persist lastError and retryCount in Job.
  - Dead-letter or alert if retryLimit reached.
- Validation Failures:
  - Immediate failure with actionable messages; do not enqueue.
- Idempotent Processing Steps:
  - Each stage writes idempotent records keyed by documentId/segmentId; reprocessing uses existing keys to avoid duplicates.

8. Testing Plan

  - Format adapters: parse and extract for sample PDFs, TXT, MD.

  - Search returns relevant results with accurate snippets.

9. Design Tradeoff
- Tradeoff: Rich per-segment indexing versus simplicity.
  - Chosen: Index at a per-segment granularity to support precise search results and meaningful snippets, accepting higher indexing complexity and storage. Rationale: users expect document-level and section-level search capabilities; per-segment indexing enables more relevant ranking and snippet generation, at the cost of additional storage and compute during indexing. Alternative is document-level indexing only, which would simplify storage but reduce search granularity and result quality.

- REST API contracts for upload, status, search, and retrieval.
- Planned project structure emphasizing maintainability for a small team.

- One design tradeoff highlighted to guide future evolution.

[Analyst]
Below is a concise, decision-grounded synthesis of the proposed design, tied directly to the task requirements. It emphasizes constraints, explicit calculations, and actionable rationale for maintainability, extensibility, and reliability.

Grounded conclusions by requirement

1) Ingestion, validation, storage, and processing
- Conclusion: A core, asynchronous pipeline with idempotent deduplication best satisfies reliability and duplicate-prevention constraints. Computing a content hash on upload enables safe upsert behavior: if a duplicate exists (and is not deleted), the system can return the existing documentId and current status without reprocessing. This directly addresses the “prevent unnecessary duplicate records” constraint.
- Key implication: Treat upload as a two-phase decision (dedupe check, then enqueue if new). Ensure the hash is computed deterministically (e.g., SHA-256 of raw bytes) and stored alongside metadata for idempotent retry behavior.

2) Supported formats and extensibility
- Conclusion: A plug-in Format Adapters abstraction (validate, extractText, segmentText) provides clean separation of concerns and enables flat addition of new formats with minimal risk to existing code. The three current adapters (PDF, TXT, Markdown) satisfy the required inputs; adding new formats later becomes a straightforward extension.
- Design impact: Adapters should expose a stable interface; the orchestrator uses the adapter determined by mimeType or file extension. This reduces branching and keeps maintenance scope small for a small team.

3) Text extraction, segmentation, and indexing
- Conclusion: Per-segment indexing (not just document-level) improves search quality and snippet relevance, aligning with user expectations for precise results. Although it increases indexing work and storage, the benefits for search UX justify the tradeoff.
- Implication: Segment metadata (range, position, possibly section headings) should be lightweight yet sufficient for ranking and snippet generation. Store per-segment index entries to support fast neighborhood queries during search.

4) End-to-end processing workflow
- Conclusion: An explicit job-based workflow with a Status & Telemetry Service is required to provide clear progress indicators and robust retry semantics. Distinguishing transient vs non-transient errors allows safe backoffs and prevents unnecessary retries on invalid content.
- Operational note: A dead-letter path or alert should trigger when retry limits are exceeded to avoid silent failures.

5) REST API design
- Conclusion: The API surface covers ingestion, status, content access, and search with a reasonable separation of concerns:
  - POST /documents for ingestion
  - GET /documents/{documentId} for metadata/status
  - GET /documents/{documentId}/contents for extracted text/segments
  - GET /documents/{documentId}/search for in-document search
  - GET /jobs/{jobId} for processing telemetry
- Rationale: This design satisfies the requirement to report processing status and support document search while remaining approachable for a small team. Optional delete/archive endpoints can be included later if needed.

6) Data model
- Conclusion: Data models align with requirements and balance clarity with maintainability:
  - Document, OriginalFile, TextContent, Segment, IndexEntry, Job
- Important constraints validated:
  - Hash field enables deduplication
  - Status field drives UI and API responses
  - Segment supports per-section granularity
- Futureproofing: Metadata stored as JSON enables easy extension for new formats without schema churn.

7) Project structure and maintainability
- Conclusion: A modular repo layout with clear boundaries between api, services, processors, indexing, jobs, and models supports small-team maintainability. The “format_adapters” directory provides a clean extensibility path for new formats with minimal cross-module impact.
- Maintaining simplicity: Keep processor logic stateless between worker restarts where feasible; rely on idempotent writes for safe retries.

8) Error handling, retry, and duplicate prevention
- Conclusion: The following mechanisms are essential and sufficient to satisfy constraints:
  - Idempotent uploads via content hash and upsert semantics
  - Distinguish retryable vs non-retryable errors with exponential backoff
  - Persist lastError, retryCount, and provide a dead-letter path at max retries
  - Validation failures fail fast with actionable messages (no enqueue)
- Operational guardrails: Ensure retry loops are bounded and have visibility through /jobs/{jobId} endpoints for operators.

9) Testing plan
- Conclusion: A layered testing plan is essential to maintain quality with a small team:
  - Unit tests for adapters, hashing, and segmenting
  - Integration tests for end-to-end flow with mocked storage/index layers
  - Contract tests for REST API inputs/outputs
  - Load testing to validate concurrency behavior and search latency
- Rationale: Early, targeted tests reduce regression risk as the team scales.

10) Design tradeoff
- Conclusion: Per-segment indexing is chosen over document-level indexing to improve snippet quality and ranking precision. This tradeoff increases indexing complexity and storage and is justified by user-centered search quality requirements.
- Alternative acknowledged: Document-level indexing would simplify implementation but degrade user experience for granular searches.

Overall recommendation
- Proceed with the described architecture as a modular backend with asynchronous processing, robust deduplication, and extensible format adapters. Keep the initial scope tight (PDF/TXT/Markdown adapters, moderate segment sizing, and a straightforward index) to deliver a maintainable baseline for a small team, with a clear path to extend formats, adjust segment granularity, and enhance indexing strategies as needed.
